data.title: 5 years on, digital banks are taking root in Hong Kong
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
In 2019, Hong Kong’s fintech development reached a milestone when the first virtual banks were granted licences. Interestingly, the earliest people around me to get their feet wet were not tech enthusiasts, but two long-time professionals from traditional finance. They left familiar careers to join digital banks, convinced that a mobile-first model could reshape banking.
I was more cautious. My doubts were not about the technology, but about business viability. In a market where banking licences are highly valuable, regulation is strict and traditional banks already dominate daily services, could these newcomers truly challenge established names?
To support my friends, I opened a virtual bank account and became an early user. The experience was undeniably smooth. With just a selfie and an ID upload, an account was opened in minutes. Yet in the first year or so, digital banks still felt like a marketing-led proposition. Offers centred on high deposit rates, cashback cards and fee-free forex spending. Service interruptions during system upgrades and the occasional instability of overseas payments made it hard to trust them with core services such as salary accounts or corporate cash management.
The turning point came in the past year, as digital banks moved beyond their novelty phase. The Hong Kong Association of Banks’ Digital Banking Education Taskforce commissioned Baptist University to conduct a survey, interviewing 1,006 members of the public and 205 small and medium-sized enterprises. Released late last year, the survey found about 93 per cent of respondents and over 98 per cent of SMEs reporting that digital banks could meet at least half of their daily banking needs. That is a meaningful shift: digital banks are no longer just tools for collecting perks, but increasingly a mainstream choice for payment, budgeting and basic wealth management.
Trust, however, rests on safety. A common misconception is that newer banks face lighter oversight. In reality, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority applies the same regulatory standards to digital and traditional banks, and digital banks are covered by the Deposit Protection Scheme, with eligible deposits protected up to HK$800,000. On top of regulation, digital banks have leveraged technology to combat increasingly sophisticated fraud, including deepfake-enabled scams. The same survey found high confidence in security measures.
Five years may be brief in banking history, but it is enough to reshape habits. I had applied for my first virtual card out of curiosity. By last year, I realised I had already come to rely on digital banks – even for tax loan services. The journey from scepticism to dependence reflects how digital banks have started to redefine banking here.
James Tsui Siu-lung, Kowloon Bay
Imposing new requirements on qualified teachers is unfair
As an English educator with 10 years of teaching experience in Hong Kong – including five as a fully vetted native-speaking English teacher (NET) – I write in response to recent letters about the new IELTS (International English Language Testing System) requirement under the NET grant. This policy is not merely inconvenient; it is methodologically flawed and retroactively unfair.
I am now being asked to submit new IELTS results despite holding a British Master’s degree and a PGCE (postgraduate certificate in education) and having been approved under the original NET scheme with those same credentials. This policy, applied without recognition of previous Education Bureau vetting, undermines professional trust.
The IELTS was explicitly designed for non-native speakers. Its descriptors, scoring norms and validation studies are based on second language acquisition. Applying it to native speakers is like using a thermometer calibrated for ice to measure fire.
The original NET scheme already ensured high standards through rigorous academic verification and in-person interviews that assessed spoken fluency, cultural awareness and teaching ability.
If the goal is authentic English proficiency, let’s reinvest in judgment by qualified assessors – not a one-size-fits-all exam never intended for this purpose.
Khalil Bendie, Causeway Bay
Bright year ahead
The importance of Hong Kong as a financial bridge between mainland China and the rest of the world and as an IPO (initial public offering) market is once again validated. This year may be even better for banks in Hong Kong, as more set up family offices and invest here.
Rishi Teckchandani, Mid-Levels

data.title: Hong Kong housing estate flat owners drop contractor linked to deadly Tai Po blaze
data.description:
Owners of a private housing estate in Tuen Mun have voted overwhelmingly to terminate their renovation contract with Prestige Construction and Engineering Company – the same contractor responsible for works at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court, in the aftermath of the deadly blaze
At a general meeting held on Friday night at Elegance Gardens, 96.45 per cent of 1,655 effective undivided shares held by flat owners voted to terminate the contract.
Several owners who spoke to the Post said they no longer trusted the company to complete the renovation safely and feared the project might be left unfinished.
“We are worried about whether the renovation would become an incomplete mess. That’s why so many flat owners came down for the meeting,” Eric Yu, who owns a flat in Block Two of Elegance Gardens, said.
The meeting drew 253 flat owners, representing 31.6 per cent of all owners in the estate.
Officials from the Buildings Department and Home Affairs Department, along with a district councillor, also took part in discussions on how to handle the estate’s existing scaffolding.
A Housing Department representative surnamed Kwok advised the owners’ committee to dismantle scaffolding around two of the estate’s three residential blocks and warned of “serious cracks” spotted on sections of the bamboo structure.
Prestige’s lawyers have accused the owners’ committee of “illegally and unreasonably” withholding a HK$700,000 (US$90,000) guarantee deposit and have demanded payment of HK$4.53 million for completed works.
Owners, however, disputed the claims and asked their lawyer and engineering consultant to assess whether Prestige’s accusations were justified.
Disgruntled owners also voiced concerns about deteriorating bamboo scaffolding and a rodent infestation across the estate.
Yu, who owns a three-bedroom flat in Block Two, said he had heard of up to three incidents of bamboo falling off scaffolding over the past week.
“Many owners have sent pictures to our WhatsApp group, showing bent bamboo sticks and some dangling in mid-air,” he said.
He added that residents on higher floors had shared photos of damaged instant noodles, suspected to be eaten by rats.
Former owners’ committee chairman Lau, a flat owner in Block Three, said he was worried about legal liability if no company stepped forward to take over the scaffolding works.

“If nobody is interested in giving us a quote, my biggest concern is the legal liability if the scaffolding shows hazards during the Lunar New Year holiday,” Lau said.
Another issue discussed was whether completing the remaining works would lead to additional costs. Renovations still required include wall repairs on the first block and waterproofing on the rooftops of all three blocks.
Yu said he had already paid HK$120,000 for his share in the renovations.
“If we need to issue a tender again and need to pay more, I believe some residents might not be able to afford it,” he said.
Prestige has been under intense scrutiny since a blaze at Wang Fuk Court on November 26, Hong Kong’s deadliest in decades, claimed at least 161 lives.
Substandard scaffolding mesh and foam boards used to cover outside the windows in the renovation project, which Prestige undertook, are believed to have accelerated the spread of the fire amid strong winds and dry conditions.
Police later arrested Hau Wa-kin and Ho Kin-yip, both directors of Prestige, and its authorised signatory Steve Wong Chung-kee on suspicion of manslaughter the day after the fire.
Authorities revealed that Prestige was undertaking 11 other renovation projects at privately owned residential estates across the city. The Buildings Department has since inspected those sites and pledged follow-up action.
The Post previously found that Prestige Construction & Engineering Co Ltd had been cited multiple times for breaching construction safety regulations.

data.title: All aboard the cat train as Japan railway gets new feline stationmaster
data.description:
A calico cat named Yontama was formally appointed stationmaster at Kishi Station in western Japan this week, stepping into a role that is at once whimsical and ceremonial but also important to the survival of a rural railway line.
The appointment ceremony on Wednesday drew fans, photographers and local officials to the station in Kinokawa, Wakayama prefecture, where Wakayama Electric Railway President Mitsunobu Kojima placed a medal around Yontama’s neck as the crowd applauded.
Yontama is the third cat to hold the title on the Kishigawa Line, following the death in November of her predecessor Nitama, who served for nearly a decade and has since been named honorary stationmaster.

The railway also introduced its newest feline recruit, Rokutama, a young calico described not as an apprentice but as a “stationmaster candidate”, a deliberately playful distinction that reflects the elaborate lore surrounding the line’s cat staff.
According to the operator’s own account, Rokutama was rescued as a kitten last spring after the company asked animal welfare contacts to alert it if a suitable calico appeared, a request that was answered within days.
Her official profile reads like a character sketch rather than a résumé: she is said to enjoy eating and playing alone, dislikes loud noises and nail clipping, and is described by staff as stubborn but not aggressive.
For now, Rokutama will work on days when the senior cats are off duty, allowing her to ease into the role.
At the ceremony, Kojima even “translated” the cat’s thoughts for the crowd, joking that she was not yet a full stationmaster and asking for patience as she learned the ropes.
Kojima told reporters he hoped Yontama would “lead the way as stationmaster at a time when local railways are at a turning point of major changes”, according to Kyodo, a comment that underscored the seriousness beneath the spectacle.
A cat that saved a railway
The Kishigawa Line first gained national attention in 2007 when a stray cat named Tama was appointed stationmaster at Kishi Station as the railway faced possible closure.
What began as a novelty quickly became a phenomenon, drawing tourists from across Japan and overseas, generating merchandise sales and transforming the station into a destination in its own right.
Researchers later estimated Tama’s economic impact at hundreds of millions of yen, and possibly more than 1 billion yen (US$6.3 million) over several years, a boost that helped keep the line afloat.
Tama’s impact was so lasting that after her death in 2015 she was commemorated at a small shrine inside the station, and her successors have inherited not just her title but her role as the railway’s most effective ambassador.

Nitama inherited the post later that year and carried it until her death last autumn, greeting passengers from a glass-fronted office that became one of the line’s most photographed spots.
Yontama, born in 2016, has effectively been in training for years, rising through a mock corporate ladder – from trainee stationmaster to “super stationmaster” – before her latest promotion to the top post at Kishi Station.
The station itself fully embraces the theme, with a cat-faced building, a cafe and a shop selling Tama-themed goods, turning an ordinary commute into a small pilgrimage for fans.
For long-time followers like Chisako Asano, a housewife from Osaka prefecture who attended this week’s ceremony, the cats are more than mascots.
“I hope the cat stationmasters will continue to watch over the Kishigawa Line,” she said, as quoted by Kyodo.
The Kishigawa Line’s success helped normalise the idea that animals could serve as living mascots, inspiring other struggling railways to appoint stationmasters of their own as a way of drawing visitors and attention.
In the Tohoku region, dogs such as Wasao and his mate Tsubaki were named tourism stationmasters on JR lines, greeting visitors and fronting local promotions.
Cats, however, remain the most enduring – and economically potent – ambassadors. On the Hitachinaka Seaside Railway, resident cats such as Osamu and Minisamu have become familiar presences on station platforms, while at Ashinomaki-Onsen Station on the Aizu Railway, successive feline stationmasters have held honorary titles.

data.title: What does HSBC’s buyout of Hang Seng Bank mean for its future and investors?
data.description:
Following the January 8 shareholder vote that overwhelmingly approved the largest privatisation deal in Hong Kong’s history, many questions remain about the future of Hang Seng Bank, its stakeholders and the broader financial landscape.
For Hang Seng Bank’s shareholders, the immediate question is what to do with the HK$106.16 billion (US$13.6 billion) windfall from HSBC Holdings for acquiring the remaining 37 per cent it does not own.
“I invested in Hang Seng Bank for the long term because of the high dividend payment,” said Cecilia Ko, who holds about 1,000 shares. “Delisting Hang Seng reduces investors’ choice to invest in a bank dedicated to the local and mainland Chinese market.”
The differences between HSBC, with its international focus, and Hang Seng Bank, which has traditionally centred on Hong Kong and China, highlight the challenges ahead on how to integrate the two internally while keeping their brand identities distinct.

Analysts expect significant restructuring at HSBC and Hang Seng in the months ahead, with the goal of integrating the two banks, reducing operating costs, enhancing synergies, and tackling Hang Seng’s growing bad-debt burden.
“This is part of a broader trend where controlling shareholders seek to streamline operations and enhance control,” said Allan Zeman, a business tycoon and founder of nightlife district Lan Kwai Fong.
Zeman, an HSBC shareholder and customer for over half a century, said the deal would give HSBC the flexibility to carry out more restructuring as Hang Seng faces headwinds from a slump in Hong Kong’s property market and rising bad debts.
“The privatisation of Hang Seng Bank is more of a transformation than a pure loss [for] minority shareholders who have been presented with a lucrative cash exit,” he told the Post in an interview. “Hang Seng’s privatisation gives HSBC greater operational freedom to manage these challenges away from quarterly earnings scrutiny.”
Aggressive lending to developers has left Hang Seng Bank vulnerable to the prolonged downturn in both residential and commercial real estate in Hong Kong, with impaired property loans surging 85 per cent year on year to HK$25 billion as of June 2025, while its first-half net profit slumped 30.5 per cent due to rising bad-debt provisions.
A JPMorgan report said some of its clients questioned HSBC’s timing for the buyout, “given heightened concerns over the asset quality of Hong Kong commercial real estate loans, and the potential for Hang Seng Bank to ‘kitchen-sink’ its exposure to such loans”.

HSBC CEO Georges Elhedery thinks otherwise, billing the transaction as a “strategic investment for growth” that was months in the making.
While he has not revealed detailed plans about Hang Seng’s future, Elhedery shared some thoughts on what comes next.
“We will preserve Hang Seng’s brand, heritage, distinct customer proposition and branch network, while investing to unlock new strengths in products, services and technology to deliver more choice and innovation for customers,” he said on October 9, when the deal was announced.
Robert Lee Wai-wang, ­a lawmaker and the chairman of Hong Kong-based Grand Finance Group, said the restructuring should lead to cost savings and increased efficiencies in the long run, but it could also result in lay-offs.
“Having a well-capitalised parent company will help Hang Seng Bank absorb losses from property loans,” Lee said.
The Hang Seng Bank deal is Elhedery’s biggest in his short time as CEO since taking over from Noel Quinn in September 2024. At US$13.6 billion, it was also the largest buyout of a Hong Kong financial services company since records began in 1980, according to data from the London Stock Exchange Group.
The sum was nine times the US$1.5 billion Elhedery said in February he would redirect from “low-return” markets to high-growth areas like Hong Kong, mainland China, Singapore and India, with wealth management as a priority.
The bet showed Elhedery’s confidence in Hong Kong, aligning with Hang Seng Bank’s focus on the city.
While the privatisation might have disappointed loyal investors, Stephen Hui Chiu-chung, chairman and CEO of Hong Kong-based broker Luk Fook Financial Services, viewed the move as a sign of HSBC’s HK$100 billion-plus commitment to Hong Kong.
“The new CEO is keen on shifting HSBC’s focus back to Hong Kong, where it has its roots and is also its fastest growing market,” Hui said.


HSBC shares fell 8.7 per cent in the first week after the buyout announcement to hit a recent low of HK$100.9 on October 17. The decline was the result of a “knee-jerk reaction”, as the deal meant HSBC would suspend share buy-backs for three quarters, according to a JPMorgan report.
HSBC’s shares, however, had bounced back 12.4 per cent to HK$124.30 since the deal’s announcement to January 8, the day of the vote, while Hang Seng Bank rose 29.3 per cent to HK$153.90 – close to the offer price of HK$155 per share. Both trounced the Hang Seng Index, which fell 2.5 per cent during the same period.
The strong share performance reflects the pro-deal investors’ belief in the transaction’s merits.
After the privatisation, Hang Seng’s wealth management and insurance businesses would remain its growth engines, said Somerley Capital, the independent financial adviser appointed by Hang Seng to assist the independent board committee in assessing HSBC’s proposal.
The growth would be supported by buoyant capital markets and southbound Stock Connect inflows, while its diversified business could cope with the downturn of the property market, it added.
“Hang Seng Bank Group is a well-established and prudently managed banking group with its own unique identity, staff, customer base and history,” Somerley said.
Hang Seng Bank, which traces its establishment to Hang Seng Ngan Ho in 1933, was sold to HSBC in 1965 after a bank run wiped out almost a quarter of its reserves.
Over time, HSBC increased its ownership of Hang Seng Bank to almost 63 per cent, from the initial 51 per cent controlling stake. Hang Seng Bank was later listed on the Hong Kong stock exchange in 1972.
While the two lenders operate separately with distinct focuses, they could share certain networks or technologies – such as their ATM systems – to serve each other’s clients.

Hang Seng Bank serves nearly four million customers and has more than 250 branches in Hong Kong and major mainland cities. Its core businesses include insurance, asset management, markets and securities services.
The lender’s namesake Hang Seng Index, launched in 1969, is synonymous with Hong Kong’s stock market.
HSBC, founded in Hong Kong 160 years ago, is the city’s biggest lender. It has over 40 million customers globally, spanning personal, corporate and institutional banking.
Elhedery said the integration would deliver both revenue and cost synergies through aligned product offerings, a deeper international network and consolidated strengths in insurance, asset management, markets and technology.
HSBC’s international network would allow Hang Seng to support its clients abroad, a capability many local rivals lack.
“After taking control of Hang Seng, the integration and cross-selling between HSBC and Hang Seng would improve, enhancing their competitiveness and making it harder for other lenders to compete,” said Kenny Ng Lai-yin, a strategist at Everbright Securities International.
Zeman said Hang Seng’s privatisation could push other banks to evaluate their own structures to better compete with the duo.
Ng said other Hong Kong banks might also benefit from the deal, as some Hang Seng shareholders might use the payout to invest in other lenders.
“Hang Seng Bank investors tend to be the ones who prefer to invest in banks that are focused on Hong Kong and the mainland, so they may not like to invest in HSBC, which is more of an international player,” he said. “Bank of China (Hong Kong), with a size and businesses similar to Hang Seng, would be the most likely candidate to attract their investment.”


At the shareholders’ meeting on Thursday, an investor surnamed Lee said he planned to allocate the money from the privatisation into shares of Bank of China and its subsidiary Bank of China (Hong Kong), as they were both “owned by the Chinese”.
HSBC has also offered leadership support to Hang Seng: seven of its most recent CEOs came from the parent group, starting with Vincent Cheng Hoi-chuen in 1998, followed by Raymond Or Ching-fai in 2005, Margaret Leung Ko May-yee in 2009, Rose Lee Wai-mun in 2012, Louisa Cheang Wai-wan in 2017 and Diana Cesar in 2021.
Current CEO Luanne Lim was transferred from HSBC to Hang Seng in October.
“I have strong confidence in HSBC’s leadership,” said Dickie Wong, executive director of research at uSmart Securities. “It has global visibility, solidified its Greater China operations and made smart moves such as asset sales.”
JPMorgan, however, questioned whether the privatisation could really deliver cost-cutting benefits to both lenders.
“Revenue synergies are uncertain, as there will be no merger of brands, and cost-cutting may be limited due to social pressure on maintaining employment,” the US bank said in a report, citing some clients.
HSBC told the Hong Kong government during the privatisation process that it would invest billions of dollars in the city and the region over the next few years and would not lay off staff.
For Ko, her wish is to see Hang Seng Bank as a listed company again in the future.
“I hope that after the privatisation, HSBC keeps Hang Seng’s unique culture and does not downsize or reshuffle staff, as that would damage staff morale,” she said. “I hope HSBC changes its strategy to realise Hang Seng’s full value by listing it again one day.”

data.title: Will X be banned in the UK? Elon Musk hits back at shutdown warnings
data.description:
Critics of X “want any excuse for censorship”, Elon Musk has claimed, as his website faces the threat of being shut down in Britain over deepfake pornography and child abuse images.
The billionaire appeared defiant on Friday night despite the outcry over reports X’s AI chatbot Grok was creating sexualised images of people, including children, at users’ request.
Pointing to claims other AI programmes created non-sexualised images of women in bikinis, he posted on X: “They want any excuse for censorship.”
Sexually manipulating images of women and children is despicable and abhorrent
British Technology Secretary Liz Kendall
Criticism of X has focused on Grok’s production of images of child abuse and manipulation of photographs of real women and girls to remove their clothes.
British Technology Secretary Liz Kendall said earlier in the week she would back regulator Ofcom if it decided to effectively block X if it failed to comply with UK laws, saying: “Sexually manipulating images of women and children is despicable and abhorrent.”
Ofcom said it was undertaking an “expedited assessment”.
Musk responded by sharing a post from US legislator Anna Paulina Luna threatening to sanction both British Prime Minister Keir Starmer and the United Kingdom if X was blocked in the country.

On Friday, X appeared to have changed Grok’s settings, with the chatbot telling users that only paid subscribers could ask it to manipulate images.
However, reports suggested this only applied to those making requests in reply to other posts, and other ways of editing or creating images, including on a separate Grok website, remained open.
Kendall said it was “totally unacceptable for Grok to allow this if you’re willing to pay for it” and added she expected an update on Ofcom’s next steps “in days, not weeks.”
Ofcom has powers under the Online Safety Act to fine businesses up to £18 million (US$24.1 million) or 10 per cent of global revenue, as well as to take criminal action.
It can also order payment providers, advertisers and internet service providers to stop working with a site, effectively banning them, though this would require agreement from the courts.
Kendall also pointed to plans to ban so-called nudification apps as part of the Crime and Policing Bill going through parliament, and said powers to criminalise the creation of intimate images without consent would come into force in the coming weeks.

data.title: Body, capsized boat found in Japan waters raise spectre of North Korean ‘ghost ships’
data.description:
A lone body discovered on a cold Japanese beach and a shattered wooden boat coated in black tar have stirred memories of the “ghost ships” that in past winters drifted across to Japan from North Korea.
The body washed ashore on Wednesday in Ishikawa prefecture, while a Japan Coast Guard vessel later recovered the capsized boat nearby, local media reported.
Authorities have yet to identify the man and are investigating whether he was linked to the vessel, which bore Korean characters and numbers. The coastguard believes the boat likely drifted from the Korean peninsula.
Japanese media described the vessel – roughly 12 metres (40 feet) long, crudely built of wood and coated in a black, tar-like substance – as resembling the ghost ships that have washed up in previous winters. Officials, however, have not confirmed its origin.
Against that backdrop, three theories have captured public imagination: that it was a North Korean fishing boat overcome by rough seas, an attempted defection gone wrong, or even a spy craft dispatched to gather intelligence or infiltrate Japan.

Each scenario had precedent, said James Brown, a professor of international relations at Temple University’s Tokyo campus – but premature conclusions should be avoided.
“All three are viable theories but I suppose the most likely explanation is that this is the body of a North Korean fisherman who got into trouble,” he told This Week in Asia.
“This is a country that we know is experiencing serious food shortages and many coastal communities there will depend on fishing.”
Those shortages often compelled fishermen to venture out even during the perilous winter season in boats that are poorly equipped or maintained, Brown said. “Inevitably, some are going to get swept further out to sea and disappear or wash up a long way away.”
Grim winter
Over the years, local authorities along the Sea of Japan coast have reported numerous such “ghost ship” incidents, particularly in winter, when strong seasonal winds can drive stricken vessels far from North Korean waters.
One of the most notorious cases came in December 2012, when a damaged wooden boat with Korean lettering was discovered off the port of Wajima in Ishikawa prefecture, about 850km (530 miles) from the Korean peninsula. Authorities found 10 decomposing bodies aboard, alongside fishing nets and equipment.
That winter proved particularly devastating for North Korean fishermen, with 11 vessels carrying 25 bodies washing ashore in just the final two months of the year. Many others were believed to have been lost at sea.
More recently, in the autumn of 2019, the Japan Coast Guard vessels clashed repeatedly with dozens of North Korean boats operating illegally near the Noto peninsula’s rich Yamatotai fishing grounds.


Footage released at the time showed patrols using water cannons to drive off intruders.
In one confrontation, a Japanese vessel collided with a North Korean fishing boat, which quickly sank. About 60 crew members were rescued, but since investigators could not verify their illegal activity, they were transferred to other North Korean boats and ordered to leave.
In 2018 alone, Tokyo said North Korean vessels were warned 5,315 times about illegal fishing in Japanese waters.
“That these poor fishermen are forced to put to sea in terrible conditions and aboard rickety wooden boats only to wash up on Japanese beaches reminds us that while North Korea is a security threat, its people are enduring a humanitarian disaster,” Brown said.
Driven by desperation
Another possibility is that the man discovered on Wednesday was attempting to defect – something that has happened before by boat.
In September 2011, nine North Koreans, including three children, were rescued off northern Japan after reportedly drifting at sea for five days. They later told authorities they had lost their bearings while trying to reach South Korea and were eventually allowed to travel to Seoul to seek asylum.
“In the winter, the Sea of Japan gets seasonal winds from the continent, making good sailing conditions for anyone hoping to escape south from North Korea,” one social media user commented under an Asahi newspaper article.
Another wrote: “Why would they try in weather this bad? It’s suicide.”
Heavy snow has blanketed the Sea of Japan coast in recent days, with temperatures falling to minus 0.5 degrees Celsius (31 degrees Fahrenheit) in Wajima on Thursday. Meteorologists warned of offshore winds and waves as high as six metres (20 feet).

Some online commentators speculated the vessel might instead have been part of a covert operation aimed at landing agents in Japan, echoing Pyongyang’s past abduction missions.
Pyongyang has admitted carrying out kidnappings of Japanese citizens during the Cold War, including the abduction of Megumi Yokota, who was 13 when she disappeared near her home in Niigata prefecture in 1977.
Tokyo officially recognises 17 abduction victims, though advocacy groups believe more than 100 people may have been taken.
Given the domestic scrutiny such cases still attract, Brown considers it unlikely Pyongyang would revive that tactic. But he noted another possibility.
“It is possible that the ship was smuggling something into Japan,” he said. “North Korea is a criminal state that relies on obtaining hard currency for its weapons programmes and it is willing to do anything to achieve that.”
Over the years, North Korean vessels have been intercepted carrying drugs, counterfeit currency, weapons and fake designer goods, all intended to fund the regime in Pyongyang. There have even been documented cases of cooperation with Japanese criminal syndicates, passing on synthetic narcotics in return for a share of the profits.

data.title: Where do lonely hearts in China go now? Late-night live streams
data.description:
For two weeks recently, most of my evenings were spent in Douyin’s live-stream section. On Douyin, TikTok’s sibling platform in China, shopping, entertainment and conversation blur into an endlessly scrollable feed.
The most prominent virtual rooms here are for shopping: live-streaming hosts talking at high speed, shouting out discounts. Then come the talent rooms, where people sing, dance, play the guitar or do anything that might keep you there for a few seconds longer. As I watched, more categories began to surface: streams offering late-night companionship and newer animated-avatar rooms where hosts never show their real faces. They don’t promise cheaper lipstick or furious dancing. What they sell is company.
I didn’t visit these rooms for any clear reason. One evening, while I was swiping past the usual noise, a calm, warm voice, reading a passage from a book I love, Albert Camus’ The Myth of Sisyphus, stopped me. It felt easier to stay than to keep scrolling.
The host’s voice had a tired huskiness, the kind you get from talking to strangers deep into the night. He said his job felt like that of Sisyphus, who has to keep pushing rocks up a hill forever. He had just a few dozen people in his room, so he tried to talk to everyone. It was pleasant and oddly comforting. I even set the alarm so I wouldn’t miss his 9pm stream.
He would sing songs I picked and read paragraphs from a novel I liked. Through the tiny screen, it really felt like I had a friend on the other side of the phone. Sometimes, I wondered if this was what loneliness looked like now: no more dramatic break-ups or grand confessions, just a quiet dependence on a scheduled voice on an app.
My peers in the post-85 demographic are smart enough to know it’s a performance, too busy to build new offline connections, and tired enough to accept such companionship as good enough for a Tuesday night. It asks so little of me: no need to dress up, commute or explain my past. I can drop in and out, buy 10 minutes of digital warmth with a small gift and close the app the moment real life intrudes.

Once, in a private chat, he dropped the stage tone and told me his story. He is 29 and barely graduated high school. He has worked factory jobs. His family cannot support him financially. Last year, he fell in love with a woman entirely online; she scammed him and disappeared, leaving him with huge debts and a broken heart.
That’s why he became a streaming host. He needed a way to pay back the money. Then he said something else that has stayed with me far longer than any detail of the scam: “For someone like me, this is the only job I can do that might actually let me jump class.”
I can’t verify how much of his story is true in a documentary sense. People edit themselves online. But when he messaged that he felt drained and needed to take time off, it made sense. To keep that room going, he had to be endlessly gentle, responsive and available. The job consumes the very emotional resources he’s trying to sell.
Another host I followed was a 22-year-old speaking through a customised character, a young fox. When you enter his room, you’re instantly jiejie – literally “big sister”, but a flirty, deferential term of endearment here for female viewers. The atmosphere is playful yet within platform regulations.
The fox boy’s peak moments are in “PK” or “Player Kill” sessions, which are among the most profitable events in live streaming and involve several hosts competing for fans’ gifts. During these sessions, he usually pits himself against fellow hosts he calls friends; in private, he told me some of their banter was scripted.


After we chatted for a while, he invited me to join one PK session. Before it started, he asked how much I had prepared. I had no idea there was a PK budget. When I asked how much he needed, he quickly said two or three “sports cars” – rather fancy virtual presents, at 120 yuan (US$17) each. I ended up sending three. The money felt like nothing in the moment, though it would show up in the bill eventually.
Those in this ecosystem know exactly how to braid loneliness, desire, fantasy and simple exhaustion at day’s end and pull you towards spending.
Among many of the Douyin viewers – students, office workers, those who have just lost their jobs or never had one – there might be a sense of being invisible. The likes of the fox boy are able to patch emotional vulnerabilities, call you jiejie and remember your username.
After two weeks in these virtual rooms, I found myself strangely exhausted. It wasn’t just the late nights. It was the realisation that so much of the on-screen companionship is genuine and manufactured at the same time. The words are soft, the voices are warm, but there is a price list discreetly running underneath.
This feels like a blueprint for how my generation is living with loneliness and insecurity. In a country where digital infrastructure is so advanced, almost every emotion can be routed through a screen and a carefully designed payment system. One person’s private way of getting through the night becomes another person’s revenue model.

data.title: China tutoring school’s promotion links civil service exam success to higher bride prices
data.description:
A Chinese civil service exam tutoring company has sparked controversy by claiming that they can help clients pass the test and then demand an additional 200,000 yuan (US$29,000) in bride price.
On December 28, a netizen posted a video claiming that a civil service exam tutoring institution in Hohhot, Inner Mongolia, northern China, had used a controversial banner to promote its business.
The Inner Mongolia branch of Beijing Chongzheng Education Consulting Co., Ltd., was established in October 2020 and is well known locally for its civil service exam training service.

In the video, the slogan “Come to Chongzheng and pass easily, ask for 200,000 yuan more in bride price” was displayed on a public bus.
By December 29, the video had been taken down and a member of staff from the institution claimed they were unaware of the promotion.
However, some netizens confirmed the authenticity of the slogan.
“It is true. I took the civil service exam in Inner Mongolia on the 29th. After the exam, a group of people was shouting the slogan through loudspeakers. I heard it myself,” said one person.

In China, the National Civil Service Exam, known as Guokao, is a highly competitive test that serves as a gateway to stable government jobs, often regarded as “golden rice bowls” for their job security and social prestige.
In some provinces the competition is especially fierce, as passing the exam is seen as a crucial step towards achieving upwards social mobility.
It is not uncommon for some graduates to stay at home for several years after university, preparing full time for the exam.

In the 2026 round of Guokao, more than 3.51 million people registered, an increase of nearly 260,000 on the previous year. The most competitive position saw a 7,591:1 ratio of applicants to available spots.
The obsession with civil service jobs also feeds into broader cultural marriage values.
Women who work as civil servants are often seen as “A-grade” in the marriage market due to the stability of their jobs and their family-oriented image; therefore, they are considered able to ask for a higher bride price.
On December 30, the Saihan District Market Supervision and Administration Bureau in Hohhot issued an official statement on its social media account, announcing that it had launched an investigation into the advertisement.

“The advertisement was found to be potentially in violation of the Advertising Law of the People’s Republic of China. The bureau has ordered the institution to immediately halt the marketing campaign and has filed a formal case. The final result will be made public in due course,” the statement read.
The video sparked controversy online.
One person said: “If this is their values and professionalism, how are they even qualified to offer civil service exam prep?”
“There is obviously a market for this kind of ad. They are just saying out loud what is usually an unspoken rule,” said another.

data.title: How ancient Chinese dental practices were often similar to those used today
data.description:
I welcomed the new year with a new crown – a dental one, that is. A routine check-up in late 2025 revealed fine cracks in one of my molars, which called for prompt intervention.
First came the filing and reshaping of the damaged tooth, accomplished with a tool that produces my least favourite sound, but amplified and reverberating quite literally inside my head.
A digital scan was then taken and sent to a laboratory, where the permanent crown would be fabricated. In the interim, a temporary crown was glued on to protect what had become a rude stump of a tooth.
At the follow-up visit, the dentist cemented the final crown in place, restoring both function and appearance. I was given strict instructions to abandon the habit that had probably caused the damage in the first place: gnawing on hard nuts and ice cubes. Unless, of course, I fancied more frequent visits to the dentist and the renewed trauma of his tiny power tool shaving bits off my teeth.
Ancient China’s approach to oral health was not so much the dreadful whine of a dentist’s drill but salt water, twigs and an assortment of curious implements and mixtures. Long before fluoridated toothpaste and electric toothbrushes, both elites and commoners addressed dental problems with considerable ingenuity.
The earliest records show that tooth decay is hardly a modern affliction. The character for cavities found on Shang dynasty (1600BC-1046BC) oracle bones depicts a tooth with a small worm inside – a vivid, if inaccurate, explanation for a condition that still plagues us today.
Archaeological finds from the tombs of figures such as Cao Cao (155-220) and the Ming dynasty’s Wanli Emperor (1563-1620) reveal that even the most powerful in China’s past suffered from severe decay, gum disease and other dental ailments.

Cleaning techniques in antiquity were creative by necessity. Long before nylon bristles, people rinsed their mouths with salt water, tea, wine or medicinal concoctions after meals.
Texts from the Western Zhou (1046BC-771BC) and Han (202BC-AD220) periods record morning and evening gargling, a routine that bears a striking resemblance to modern oral hygiene. Sun Simiao (581-682), the celebrated Tang dynasty doctor, prescribed a saltwater swill at dawn to keep teeth firm and gums strong.
Before modern toothbrushes appeared in China, there were chewing sticks, usually made from willow twigs or other plants. Chewed until frayed, their fibres scrubbed teeth in much the same way as a primitive brush. Archaeologists have unearthed bone toothbrush handles from burials dating to the northern Liao state (916-1125), complete with traces of bristles resembling later designs.

More direct tools were also employed. Finger-and-cloth cleaning methods appear in medical literature, and early wooden or bone toothpicks were used to dislodge food debris.
Relief from dental pain was no less inventive. Physicians used acupuncture for toothaches and experimented with fillings made from medicinal pastes.
Between the seventh and 13th centuries, texts mention arsenic compounds and amalgams containing mercury for treating cavities – deeply unsettling by today’s safety standards, but notable for addressing underlying causes rather than merely dulling pain.
China’s dental past reflects a sustained attention to oral care long before industrial toothbrushes and mass-produced toothpaste. What we now take for granted – brushing and rinsing – was already part of daily life centuries ago. Today, we add flossing and regular, if reluctant, visits to the dentist to the routine.
In the sweep of human history, the quest for healthy teeth has been as persistent as plaque itself. Ancient Chinese dental practices remind us that a good swill of salt water and a gnawed twig are not quite as far removed from our hi-tech solutions as we might like to think.

data.title: India overtakes Japan in economic size, but prosperity trails far behind
data.description:
India ushered in the new year with a symbolic triumph as its economy edged past Japan’s to become the world’s fourth largest by nominal gross domestic product.
The achievement unleashed a wave of national pride in New Delhi and among business leaders, but behind the celebrations lingers a question: can the world’s fastest-growing economy turn rapid growth into widespread prosperity?
Economists warn that GDP figures alone cannot create jobs, lift incomes or bridge yawning inequality in a nation of more than 1.4 billion people.
The challenge now is maintaining momentum and continuing to drive reforms of taxes, labour rules and foreign investment norms to turn statistical success into tangible gains for its young, still-poor population.

For Kunal Gupta, these reforms have unfolded on the factory floor. As CEO of electric-bike maker EMotorad, he has seen a fundamental shift in how businesses approach manufacturing.
“We are constantly seeing people trying to get into manufacturing under the Make in India campaign. Businesses are taking the harder step to localisation,” he said, referencing a government drive to boost a sector that still punches below its weight compared to China and other regional competitors such as Vietnam and Thailand.
India’s recent overhaul of its goods and services tax and introduction of simplified labour laws after years of delays should help improve the business climate, Gupta said.
His company, once reliant on imported parts, opened a manufacturing plant in Pune, Maharashtra state, to produce batteries and chargers locally, helped by the government’s Production Linked Incentive scheme introduced during the pandemic to reward increased domestic output.
But he says demand for his e-bikes has been blunted by a dearth of export incentives, high logistics costs compared to regional rivals and limited access to credit, despite the spread of digital finance through the government-backed Unified Payments Interface.
Faster growth needed
Analysts warn that India’s demographic advantage – more than a quarter of its people are aged between 10 and 26 – could become a liability if growth fails to create well-paid jobs.
The country must create at least 8 million jobs a year over the next decade to realise its goal of becoming a developed nation by 2047, according to the government’s chief economic adviser V. Anantha Nageswaran, who has also called for manufacturing’s share of GDP to be lifted.
“The biggest challenge, apart from India’s size, is that the external environment will not be as benign over the next 10 to 20 years,” he said last year at Columbia University’s India Summit 2025.
Despite projections that India will remain the fastest-growing G20 economy, its per capita GDP of around US$2,700 in 2024 was roughly 12 times lower than Japan’s and 20 times below Germany’s.
India needs to grow faster for longer
N.R. Bhanumurthy, economist
“India needs to grow faster for longer,” said N.R. Bhanumurthy, director of the Chennai-based Madras School of Economics, adding that “potential growth” should be “nothing less than 7.5 per cent”.
The World Bank projects growth of 6.5 per cent this year while Moody’s forecasts a similar pace through at least 2027.
Those numbers could help India move towards its goal of becoming the world’s third-largest economy by 2030 – and a “developed nation” by the centenary of its independence in 2047 – but only if reforms continue and productivity rises.
External pressures have sharpened India’s reform focus, especially since Washington imposed import tariffs as high as 50 per cent last year.

These changes include Delhi’s recent rationalisation of the goods and services tax – cutting the top rate for some goods from 28 to 18 per cent – and new labour laws that make hiring and firing easier.
Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s government has also fully opened up the insurance sector to foreign investors, removing previous investment caps, and is reviewing customs rules that currently penalise domestic manufacturers by taxing inputs more heavily than finished goods.
“Many more reforms are in the pipeline,” Bhanumurthy said. “All should help [with growth].”
Multinational companies have taken notice. Financial incentives introduced since the pandemic have drawn major manufacturers such as Apple and its suppliers, which have ramped up iPhone production in India.
Yet manufacturing still contributes only about 16 to 17 per cent of GDP, lagging behind the East Asian average of 20 to 25 per cent.
“The biggest challenges are accelerating private investments, including foreign investments; maintaining the growth in consumption; and making sure that more people get decent jobs,” Amitendu Palit, a senior research fellow at the National University of Singapore’s Institute of South Asian Studies, told This Week in Asia.
India’s currency, the rupee, hit a record low against the US dollar late last year, cushioning exporters against US tariffs.
The South Asian nation’s large domestic market and expanding ties with Europe, China and the Gulf should further limit the fallout from Washington’s trade measures, say analysts like Jamus Lim, an associate professor of economics at ESSEC Business School.
Fixing the foundations
Agriculture is India’s largest source of employment but lags in productivity. Nearly half of all workers depend on it, yet crop yields are often only around half those in more advanced economies.
Biswajit Dhar of the Council for Social Development said stronger agricultural research and innovation were essential.
“Why can’t the government strengthen existing agricultural research institutions and encourage them to produce more high-yielding varieties and help farmers get more yield?” he asked, citing the success of the “Green Revolution” of the 1960s that made India self-sufficient in grain production.
Dhar also pointed to the “White Revolution”, a dairy cooperative initiative that turned India into the world’s largest producer of milk, as proof of how inclusive models can transform rural livelihoods.

Boosting earnings from agriculture would ripple through the entire economy, Dhar said.
“If we put more money in the hands of people, then this demand problem will disappear,” he said, stressing that more needed to be done about “huge inequality” in the country.
“Poorer sections [of society] are not going to be standing on the sidelines and let people get richer.”
Economists stress that growth alone cannot bridge the inequality gap without deep investment in health, education and human capital. Studies show wide gaps between rural and urban access to quality schooling and healthcare, even as government schemes make incremental progress.
Medical insurance providing up to 500,000 rupees (US$5,600) per family for hospitalisation and tertiary care has narrowed disparities, but a yawning gulf remains, particularly in rural districts, according to The Lancet’s annual Global Burden of Disease study.
Vast variation also persists in school quality between provinces, despite improvements made through initiatives such as the New Education Policy and the Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (Education for All Movement) programme, aimed at universal elementary education.

India “has to continue improving basic educational attainment for its large working-age population, and better integrate this workforce into global supply chains”, said Lim of ESSEC Business School.
He also called for greater support for small and medium-sized enterprises, lower capital costs and a stable rupee.
Sushant Sarin, head of strategy and commercial risk solutions for India at professional services firm Aon, said Delhi must strengthen supply chain resilience against disruptions “linked to climate impact, logistics bottlenecks and infrastructure gaps”.
He added that cyber readiness and technology governance would also become vital to competitiveness as industries digitalise.
Despite the obstacles, India’s business climate is changing in ways that executives find tangible.
A continuous focus on execution would definitely unlock more value
Sarvadnya Kulkarni, Indian manufacturing executive
Sarvadnya Kulkarni, CEO of General Instruments Consortium, a leading manufacturer of industrial gauges and sensors, credited recent labour and logistics reforms with removing friction from everyday operations.
“A continuous focus on execution would definitely unlock more value,” he told This Week in Asia. “But I would say the systems that have been put in place are really good.”
Dr Satya Ramani Vadlamani, founder of pharmaceutical products manufacturer Murli Krishna Pharma, said the “bureaucratic maze” that existed two decades ago had become far simpler to navigate.
Simplified tax procedures, for instance, now mean refunds are processed within a month – something that would have previously been unimaginable.
“We can’t expect things to change overnight,” she added. “We still have problems dealing with bureaucracy, but at least things are changing.”
